title: Visas for Medical Treatment in Korea (C-3-3 and G-1-10)
content: South Korea offers dedicated visa categories for international patients traveling for medical care. The C-3-3 medical tourism visa is a short-term visa for patients visiting Korea for medical treatment or a health checkup, typically allowing stays of up to 90 days; accompanying family members or caregivers can usually apply under the same category. For treatment that requires a longer stay — such as major surgery with extended recovery, transplant follow-up, or ongoing oncology care — the G-1-10 (miscellaneous, medical) status allows long-term stays beyond 90 days and can often be applied for or extended from within Korea. To apply for a medical visa, patients generally need: a valid passport, a completed visa application, an appointment confirmation or treatment plan issued by the Korean hospital (or an invitation issued through a registered foreign-patient facilitation agency), proof of ability to cover treatment and stay costs, and in some cases medical records supporting the planned treatment. Citizens of many countries can enter Korea visa-free for short stays (often with a K-ETA electronic travel authorization) and may receive outpatient treatment or checkups during such a visit, but visa-free entry rules vary by nationality and change over time. Requirements differ by country and by Korean embassy, so always confirm current requirements with the Korean embassy or consulate in your country, or via the official Hi Korea (immigration) and Medical Korea (KHIDI) websites, before booking travel. KmedTour coordinators can help you obtain the hospital appointment confirmation needed for the visa application, but visa issuance decisions rest solely with the Korean immigration authorities.

title: Travel and Arrival Logistics for Your Medical Trip to Korea
content: Most international patients arrive through Incheon International Airport (ICN) near Seoul, which has direct flights from major hubs in Africa, the Middle East, and Southeast Asia. For treatment in other cities, Gimhae (Busan) and other regional airports connect through Incheon or via short domestic flights and the KTX high-speed rail network. Patients should plan to arrive at least one to two days before their first hospital appointment to recover from travel and complete any pre-visit preparation. Many large Korean hospitals operate international healthcare centers that can arrange airport pickup, interpretation, and on-site help with admission paperwork; availability varies by hospital, so coordination before arrival matters. Accommodation options near major hospitals range from hospital-affiliated guest houses and medical hotels to standard hotels and serviced apartments for longer recovery stays; booking near the treating hospital reduces daily travel during outpatient phases. For mobile connectivity, travelers commonly use an eSIM, a local prepaid SIM, or pocket Wi-Fi rented at the airport. Korea uses 220V power with type C/F plugs. The local currency is the Korean won (KRW); major cards are widely accepted, but some clinics request deposits or payments by bank transfer. KmedTour helps coordinate these logistics — airport pickup, interpreter scheduling, and accommodation suggestions near your hospital — as part of trip planning.

title: Paying for Medical Treatment in Korea as an International Patient
content: International patients in Korea normally pay hospitals directly, and most hospitals quote prices in Korean won. Common payment methods are international credit or debit cards and bank wire transfer; large planned procedures often require a deposit in advance, with the balance settled before discharge. Quotes from Korean hospitals are typically itemized estimates rather than fixed prices: the final bill can change with the actual length of stay, additional tests, or changes to the treatment plan, so patients should budget a margin above the quoted figure. Treatment for international patients is generally not covered by Korean national health insurance; patients relying on international health insurance or a government-sponsored treatment program should confirm coverage and the hospital's billing process with both the insurer or sponsor and the hospital's international office before traveling. Patients from countries with currency transfer limits should arrange foreign-exchange approval and transfers well before the treatment date, since international wires can take several business days. Always request an official itemized estimate and receipts from the hospital — these are also useful for visa applications, insurance claims, and customs declarations. KmedTour provides transparent itemized cost estimates covering the procedure, hospital stay, interpreter support, and travel components, and flags any quote that falls outside typical ranges for human review.

title: Recovery, Aftercare, and Follow-up After Treatment in Korea
content: Recovery planning is part of every medical trip to Korea. Before booking flights home, patients should ask the treating hospital how many days of in-country recovery and follow-up visits the procedure requires — common examples range from a few days after dental or dermatology procedures to several weeks after major orthopedic or transplant surgery. Flying too soon after surgery can be unsafe; surgeons set the fit-to-fly timeline, and airlines may require a medical certificate for recent surgical patients. Before discharge, request a complete English-language medical report: the discharge summary, operation record, prescriptions with generic drug names, and imaging on disc or via secure transfer. These documents let a doctor in your home country continue care safely. Korean hospitals can usually schedule remote follow-up consultations or coordinate with a local physician for suture removal, rehabilitation, and medication adjustments after you return home. If complications appear after returning home — fever, unusual pain, wound changes — contact a local doctor or emergency service first, then inform the Korean hospital's international office so the treating team can advise. KmedTour supports aftercare coordination: scheduling remote follow-ups with the Korean hospital, translating medical reports, and keeping your records organized for continuity of care. KmedTour itself does not provide medical advice; all clinical decisions stay with licensed physicians.

title: How KmedTour Patient Intake Works — Step by Step
content:
The KmedTour patient intake process connects international patients with verified Korean hospitals through a structured journey: submit, match, quote, book, and travel. Here is how each stage works.

1. Submit your inquiry. Create a free account on KmedTour and submit a medical inquiry from your patient dashboard. You describe your treatment goal, share any relevant medical records or reports, and indicate your target travel window. The more detail you provide, the more accurately we can match you and the faster hospitals can produce quotes. You do not need a formal referral from a home-country doctor to start.

2. Coordinator review. A KmedTour medical coordinator reviews your inquiry within one business day. They confirm that the information is sufficient to send to hospitals, flag any missing details (for example, imaging formats hospitals require), and pre-screen your case for compatibility with our partner hospitals' specialties.

3. Hospital matching and quote request. We submit your anonymized case to the Korean hospitals whose specialty, accreditation, and language-service capacity match your needs. Hospitals respond with itemized estimates, typically within a few business days. Estimates cover the procedure itself, the expected hospital stay, interpretation services, and any required pre-treatment consultations.

4. Quote review and consultation. You receive the hospital quotes through your KmedTour dashboard, with coordinator notes on each option. For complex cases or when you want to speak with a hospital's specialist before deciding, we can schedule a remote pre-consultation — by video call or written Q&A via the hospital's international healthcare center.

5. Booking and deposit. Once you choose a hospital and confirm your treatment dates, KmedTour assists with the formal booking. Most Korean hospitals require a deposit to hold your procedure date; the amount varies by hospital and procedure type. Your coordinator provides a structured payment schedule and confirms what the deposit covers.

6. Pre-travel preparation. In the weeks before departure, your coordinator helps with: obtaining the hospital appointment confirmation letter needed for a Korean medical visa (C-3-3 or G-1-10); preparing a translation of your medical records if needed; selecting accommodation near the hospital; and confirming interpreter scheduling for your admission and procedure dates.

7. Arrival and hospital admission. On arrival in Korea, KmedTour coordinators remain reachable for in-country questions. The hospital's international healthcare center handles the formal admission process, and your assigned interpreter meets you at the hospital. KmedTour remains available for logistical coordination throughout your stay.

8. Discharge and aftercare. Before you leave Korea, your coordinator confirms that you have an English-language discharge summary, imaging on disc or secure transfer, and a follow-up plan. We can schedule remote post-treatment check-ins with the Korean hospital after you return home.

KmedTour does not charge patients a fee for the intake, matching, or coordination service, and works with hospitals on a referral basis. All clinical decisions — diagnosis, treatment plan, surgical approach — are made solely by your treating Korean physicians, not by KmedTour.

title: About KmedTour — Services, Process, and How to Get Started
content:
KmedTour is a medical tourism service that helps international patients access Korean healthcare. KmedTour matches patients to vetted Korean hospitals and specialist clinics based on their treatment needs, budget, and travel timeline.

What KmedTour provides:
- Hospital and clinic matching from a curated database of Korean hospitals and specialist clinics
- Coordination support: helping patients submit inquiries, receive itemized quotes, and manage their treatment journey
- Information about visa requirements, travel logistics, and typical cost ranges
- Connection to medical coordinators who assist with scheduling and paperwork

What KmedTour does NOT do:
- KmedTour cannot provide medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment recommendations
- KmedTour cannot guarantee medical outcomes or specific procedure pricing
- KmedTour is not a licensed medical provider; all clinical decisions rest with your treating Korean physicians

How to get started:
1. Submit a patient intake form at kmedtour.com/patient-intake, providing your treatment interest, country, and contact details
2. A KmedTour medical coordinator reviews your case and matches you to suitable hospitals
3. You receive a quote package from matched hospitals through your dashboard
4. Your coordinator assists with next steps: appointment scheduling, visa guidance, and travel logistics

KmedTour accepts patients from all countries, supporting patients from Asia, the Middle East, Africa, the Americas, and Europe. For urgent questions, use the 'Contact' page or speak with a coordinator. KmedTour itself does not provide medical advice; all clinical decisions stay with licensed physicians.
